questions.question: Define data structures and classify them with examples. (5 marks)
keyPoints: A data structure is a specialized format for organizing, storing, and managing data efficiently.
Primitive: int, float, char, boolean - basic types provided by languages.
Non-primitive Linear: Arrays (fixed-size), Linked Lists (dynamic), Stacks (LIFO), Queues (FIFO).
Non-primitive Non-linear: Trees (hierarchical), Graphs (network of nodes).

questions.question: Explain the concept of Abstract Data Type (ADT) with examples. (5 marks)
keyPoints: An ADT defines a data type by its behavior (operations) rather than implementation.
Components: Data stored, Operations allowed, Implementation hidden from user.
Stack ADT: push(), pop(), peek(), isEmpty().
Queue ADT: enqueue(), dequeue(), front(), isEmpty().

questions.question: Compare and contrast linear and non-linear data structures. Give examples and explain when to use each. (10 marks)
keyPoints: Linear: Elements in sequential order, each has unique predecessor/successor (except first/last).
Non-linear: Elements not sequential, one element can connect to multiple elements.
Linear examples: Arrays (random access), Linked Lists (dynamic), Stacks (LIFO), Queues (FIFO).
Non-linear examples: Trees (hierarchical data like file systems), Graphs (networks like social media).
Use linear when data is sequential; non-linear when representing hierarchies or networks.

questions.question: Explain time complexity with common Big-O notations and their examples. (5 marks)
keyPoints: Time complexity measures algorithm performance as a function of input size.
O(1): Constant - accessing array element by index.
O(n): Linear - searching in unsorted array.
O(log n): Logarithmic - binary search in sorted array.
O(n^2): Quadratic - bubble sort, nested loops.
